extra.message: User input from request.POST.get('name') and request.POST.get('pass') is concatenated directly into a raw SQL string passed to login.objects.raw(). An attacker can inject arbitrary SQL via the 'name' or 'pass' parameters to bypass authentication or exfiltrate data.

User input from request.POST.get('name') and request.POST.get('pass') is concatenated directly into a raw SQL string passed to sql_lab_table.objects.raw() at line 878. This allows SQL injection to bypass authentication or extract data from the database.

User input from request.POST.get('domain') is inserted into a shell command string via format() and passed to subprocess.Popen() with shell=True. The regex-based protocol stripping on line 420 is trivially bypassable (e.g., using '; malicious_command' or '| malicious_command'). This allows arbitrary OS command execution.

User input from request.POST.get('val') is passed directly to Python's eval() function at line 460. This allows an attacker to execute arbitrary Python code on the server, leading to full system compromise.

User input from request.POST.get('expression') is passed directly to Python's eval() at line 218. The view also has @csrf_exempt and no authentication decorator, meaning unauthenticated users can execute arbitrary Python code remotely.

User input from request.POST.get('ip') is concatenated directly into a shell command string 'nmap ' + ip and passed to subprocess.Popen() with shell=True via command_out(). An attacker can inject arbitrary OS commands (e.g., '127.0.0.1; rm -rf /').

User-controlled cookie value 'token' is base64-decoded and passed to pickle.loads() at line 214. An attacker can craft a malicious pickled object in the cookie to achieve arbitrary code execution on the server.
User-supplied form input 'serialized_data' is base64-decoded and passed to pickle.loads() at line 36. This allows arbitrary code execution via crafted pickle payloads.

XML parser is created with feature_external_ges set to True at line 259, and user-supplied request body is parsed at line 260. The endpoint also has @csrf_exempt. This enables XXE attacks allowing an attacker to read arbitrary files from the server or perform SSRF.

User-supplied URL from request.POST['url'] is passed directly to requests.get() at line 963 without any validation or URL allowlisting. An attacker can use this to make requests to internal services (e.g., http://127.0.0.1, http://metadata.google.internal, etc.) or access cloud metadata endpoints.

User input from request.POST['blog'] is used to construct a file path via os.path.join() and opened for reading at line 927. An attacker can use path traversal sequences like '../../etc/passwd' to read arbitrary files from the server filesystem.

User-supplied blog content from request.POST['blog'] is written directly to a Django template file at line 996 and then rendered via render() at line 1006. The filter_blog() function at utility.py:56 is a no-op (returns input unchanged). This allows Server-Side Template Injection (SSTI), enabling attackers to execute arbitrary code via Django template tags like {% load %} or {% include %}.

User-uploaded YAML file is loaded using yaml.load() with yaml.Loader (the unsafe full Loader) at line 560. This allows arbitrary Python code execution via YAML deserialization attacks (e.g., !!python/object/apply:os.system). yaml.safe_load() should be used instead.

User-supplied function string from request.POST.get('function') is passed directly to ImageMath.eval() at line 588. ImageMath.eval() can execute arbitrary Python expressions, allowing remote code execution.

User input from request.GET.get('q') is passed as 'query' to the template xss_lab.html where it is rendered with the |safe filter (xss_lab.html:27), bypassing Django's auto-escaping. This allows reflected XSS via the 'q' GET parameter.

User input from request.POST.get('username') is passed as 'username' to xss_lab_2.html where it is rendered with the |safe filter (xss_lab_2.html:20). The blacklist filter on line 112 only removes tags, which is trivially bypassable (e.g., using ,
User input from request.GET['username'] is passed to the template lab2.html where it is rendered with the |safe filter (lab2.html:11). This allows reflected XSS via the 'username' GET parameter.

Admin access is controlled by a client-side cookie value request.COOKIES.get('admin') == '1' at line 347. An attacker can trivially set this cookie to '1' in their browser to gain admin access and view the secret key without providing valid admin credentials.
Admin access in a1_broken_access_lab_1 is controlled by a client-side cookie value request.COOKIES.get('admin') == '1' at line 758. An attacker can set this cookie to gain admin privileges.
Admin access in a1_broken_access_lab_2 is controlled by checking if User-Agent header equals 'pygoat_admin' at line 797. An attacker can trivially spoof the User-Agent header to gain admin access.

The a1_broken_access_lab3_secret view at line 837 serves a secret page with only authentication check but no authorization check. Any authenticated user can access the secret endpoint meant for admin users only, as noted by the comment 'no checking applied here'.

The auth_lab_login view at line 302 uses the 'userid' cookie (an auto-incrementing integer) to look up user accounts without any verification. An attacker can change the userid cookie value to access other users' accounts (IDOR).

cwe: CWE-639: Authorization Bypass Through User-Controlled Key

extra.message: The xxe_parse view is decorated with @csrf_exempt at line 255. This endpoint parses user-supplied XML and updates the database. Combined with the XXE vulnerability, the lack of CSRF protection makes it exploitable from any website via cross-origin requests.

The csrf_transfer_monei_api view handles money transfers via GET request at line 192 with no CSRF protection. An attacker can craft a malicious link like '/mitre/9/lab/api/attacker/1000' and trick a victim into clicking it to transfer funds.

Django SECRET_KEY is hardcoded in the settings file: 'lr66%-a!$km5ed@n5ug!tya5bv!0(yqwa1tn!q%0%3m2nh%oml'. This key is used for cryptographic signing of sessions, cookies, and CSRF tokens. If compromised, an attacker can forge sessions and CSRF tokens.

JWT secret key SECRET_COOKIE_KEY is hardcoded as 'PYGOAT' at line 171. This weak, short key is used for signing JWT cookies in sec_misconfig_lab3. An attacker can easily brute-force or guess this key and forge JWT tokens to gain admin access.

JWT secret key is hardcoded as 'csrf_vulneribility' at line 169 in csrf_lab_login. This key is used for signing JWT cookies for authentication in the CSRF lab. An attacker can forge JWT tokens for any user.
Hardcoded credentials: username 'jack' and password 'jacktheripper' are hardcoded at line 766 in the a1_broken_access_lab_1 view function.
Hardcoded credentials: username 'John' with password 'reaper' and 'admin' with password 'admin_pass' are hardcoded at lines 831-833 in a1_broken_access_lab_3 view.
Flask secret key is hardcoded as 'your-secret-key-here' at line 8, and user credentials including admin password 'admin123' and user password 'password123' are hardcoded in plaintext at lines 11-22.

Django DEBUG mode is set to True in production settings. This exposes detailed error pages with stack traces, settings values, and local variables to any user, which aids attackers in finding additional vulnerabilities.

cwe: CWE-209: Information Exposure Through Error Message

extra.message: Django DEBUG mode is set to True. Combined with ALLOWED_HOSTS = ['*'] at line 13, this exposes detailed error pages to any user from any host.

cwe: CWE-209: Information Exposure Through Error Message

extra.message: Django SECRET_KEY is hardcoded as 'django-insecure-key-for-demonstration-only' at line 8. This key is used for cryptographic signing.

User password is stored in plaintext at line 287. authLogin.objects.create() stores the password directly from request.POST['pass'] without any hashing. This exposes all user passwords if the database is compromised.

OTP is generated as a 3-digit random number (range 100-999) at line 496. This is only 900 possible values, making it trivially brute-forceable. Combined with no rate limiting, an attacker can enumerate all OTPs to bypass authentication for any account including admin@pygoat.com.

cwe: CWE-330: Use of Insufficiently Random Values

extra.message: The all_users_data_view function at line 112 exposes all users' sensitive data (credit cards, SSNs, API keys) via a JSON API endpoint with no authentication or authorization checks. Any unauthenticated user can access all users' PII.

cwe: CWE-306: Missing Authentication for Critical Function

extra.message: Sensitive data flag 'FLAGTHATNEEDSTOBEFOUND' is hardcoded in the Django settings file at line 27. With DEBUG=True, this value is exposed through Django's error pages which display all settings.

User-supplied code from request.POST.get('log_code') and request.POST.get('api_code') is written directly to Python files at lines 69-74 (playground/A9/main.py and playground/A9/api.py). The endpoint has @csrf_exempt and no authentication. This allows an attacker to overwrite Python module files with arbitrary code that will be executed by Django on import.

cwe: CWE-94: Improper Control of Generation of Code

extra.message: User-supplied code from request.POST.get('code') is written directly to playground/A6/utility.py at lines 132-135. The endpoint has @csrf_exempt and no authentication decorator. This allows arbitrary code injection by overwriting a Python module that is imported elsewhere.

cwe: CWE-94: Improper Control of Generation of Code

extra.message: Session token is created by base64-encoding 'username:datetime' at line 45. An attacker can easily forge session tokens for any user by base64-encoding 'admin:any_datetime' to impersonate the admin user (validated at line 111).

Authentication cookie for crypto_failure_lab3 is constructed as plaintext 'username|expiry_datetime' at line 1074 and set without encryption or signing at line 1076. An attacker can forge 'admin|future_date' to gain admin access (verified at line 1060).

Cookie is set with samesite=None and secure=False at line 291. The SameSite=None attribute requires the Secure flag to be set. Without it, the cookie may not be sent in cross-site requests in modern browsers, but the combination indicates a security misconfiguration.

cwe: CWE-614: Sensitive Cookie in HTTPS Session Without 'Secure' Attribute

extra.message: User-controlled input (username from request.POST and ip from X-Forwarded-For header) is interpolated directly into log messages via f-strings at lines 668-672. An attacker can inject fake log entries by including newline characters in the username or X-Forwarded-For header, potentially hiding malicious activity or creating false audit trails.

cwe: CWE-117: Improper Output Neutralization for Logs
